coredata.dc:title: Detection of yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae with ionic liquid mediated carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jia-Li
Teng, Ji-Yuan
Jia, Te
Shu, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Hydrophobic nitrogen-doped carbon dots are prepared with energetic ionic liquid (1,3-dibutylimidazolium dicyandiamide, BbimDCN) as carbon source. A yield of as high as 58% is obtained for the carbon dots, shortly termed as BbimDCN-OCDs, due to the presence of thermal-instable N(CN)2 - moiety. BbimDCN-OCDs exhibit favorable biocompability and excellent imaging capacity for fluorescence labelling of yeast cell Saccharomyces cerevisiae. In addition, chitosan-modified Dy3+-doped magnetic nanoparticles (shortly as Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4) with superparamagnetism are prepared. The electrostatic attraction between positively charged magnetic nanoparticles and negatively charged yeast cells facilitates exclusive recognition/isolation of S. cerevisiae. In practice, S. cerevisiae is labelled by BbimDCN-OCDs and adhered onto the Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4. The yeast/ BbimDCN-OCDs/Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 composite is then isolated with an external magnet and the fluorescence from BbimDCN-OCDs incorporated in S. cerevisiae is monitored. The fluorescence intensity is linearly correlated with the content of yeast cell, showing a calibration graph of F = 3.01log[C]+11.7, offering a detection limit of 5×102 CFU/mL. S. cerevisiae content in various real sample matrixes are quantified by using this protocol.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ionic liquid
Carbon dots
Magnetic nanoparticles
Microbial detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell imaging and growth curve of yeast cell incubated with BbimDCN-OCDs
Preparation of chitosan-modified Dy3+-doped ferrite nanoparticles
Characterizations of ionic liquid mediated BbimDCN-OCDs
Labelling of yeast cells with BbimDCN-OCDs
Labelling and detection of S. cerevisiae
Bacteria quantification in real sample matrixes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM image of BbimDCN-OCDs (A); UV–vis absorption spectra (10μg/mL in ethanol) and fluorescence emission spectra of (20μg/mL in ethanol) of BbimDCN-OCDs (B).
FT-IR spectra of BbimDCN and BbimDCN-OCDs (A); XPS spectra of BbimDCN-OCDs (B); high resolution XPS spectra of C1s (C) and N1s (D).

Dark field (A) and bright field (B) fluorescence images of S. cerevisiae cells after incubation with BbimDCN-OCDs at 28°C for 30min. Bi, Bii, and Biii are obtained by merging B with Ai, Aii, and Aiii, respectively. Excitation wavelengths at 405nm (i), 488nm (ii) and 561nm (iii).

Fluorescence spectra of S. cerevisiae (1×104-1×106 CFU/mL) with labelling of BbimDCN-OCDs (A), and with labelling of BbimDCN-OCDs in addition to enrichment by magnetic Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 (B). The inset is the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and the cell content.
The effect of BbimDCN-OCDs at various concentration levels on the growth of S. cerevisiae.
HR-TEM image of Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 (A); FT-IR spectra of chitosan, Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 and Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 (B).
Magnetization curves (A) and Zeta potentials (B) of Fe3O4, Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 and Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4.
Schematic illustration for the labelling of Saccharomyces cerevisiae with carbon dots BbimDCN-OCDs, retention and isolation by the magnetic Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4, and fluorescence detection.
Analytical results for tap water and apple juice.

head.ce:title: Detection of yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae with ionic liquid mediated carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Northeastern University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Northeastern University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrophobic nitrogen-doped carbon dots are prepared with energetic ionic liquid (1,3-dibutylimidazolium dicyandiamide, BbimDCN) as carbon source. A yield of as high as 58% is obtained for the carbon dots, shortly termed as BbimDCN-OCDs, due to the presence of thermal-instable N(CN)2 - moiety. BbimDCN-OCDs exhibit favorable biocompability and excellent imaging capacity for fluorescence labelling of yeast cell Saccharomyces cerevisiae. In addition, chitosan-modified Dy3+-doped magnetic nanoparticles (shortly as Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4) with superparamagnetism are prepared. The electrostatic attraction between positively charged magnetic nanoparticles and negatively charged yeast cells facilitates exclusive recognition/isolation of S. cerevisiae. In practice, S. cerevisiae is labelled by BbimDCN-OCDs and adhered onto the Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4. The yeast/ BbimDCN-OCDs/Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 composite is then isolated with an external magnet and the fluorescence from BbimDCN-OCDs incorporated in S. cerevisiae is monitored. The fluorescence intensity is linearly correlated with the content of yeast cell, showing a calibration graph of F = 3.01log[C]+11.7, offering a detection limit of 5×102 CFU/mL. S. cerevisiae content in various real sample matrixes are quantified by using this protocol.

Hydrophobic nitrogen-doped carbon dots are prepared with energetic ionic liquid (1,3-dibutylimidazolium dicyandiamide, BbimDCN) as carbon source. Saccharomyces cerevisiae is labelled by BbimDCN-OCDs and adhered onto the chitosan-modified Dy3+-doped magnetic nanoparticles. The yeast/BbimDCN-OCDs/Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 composite is then isolated with an external magnet and the fluorescence from BbimDCN-OCDs incorporated in S. cerevisiae is monitored.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Yeast refers to a variety of single-cell fungi that can ferment sugars and it is widely used for brewing production and scientific studies including genetic engineering and cell cycles. However, as conditional pathogens, yeasts can be particularly susceptible to infection in patients with low immunity. Candida albicans is able to cause urethritis [1] and even Saccharomyces cerevisiae has the potential to cause diseases [2]. Therefore, sensitive and selective detection of yeasts is of great significance, and traditional methods are based primarily on microbiological and biochemical analysis, e.g., ATP enzyme bioluminescence, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [3,4]. These approaches are usually time consuming with low efficiency, and are difficult to realize real-time detection.

For the purpose of achieving high sensitivity and efficiency, a few nanomaterial-based fluorescence spectrophotometric methods were developed in recent years. As a new type of fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) contain a carbon core and surface functional groups. CDs offer a series of advantages, e.g., high quantum yield, low toxicity, favorable biocompatibility and cost effective. The feasibility of microbiological labelling and detection with CDs as multicolor labelling agents have recently been demonstrated, by serving as fluorescent probes in cellular imaging and bacteria detection, e.g., Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus [5–7], and achieving direct yeast cell (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) labelling by cell endocytosis [8]. A mesoporous silica oxide micro-particles, e.g., meso-SiO2/CDs complex, is found to be highly biocompatible for yeast cells and thus can be used for yeast detection [9].

Ionic liquids (ILs) consist of organic cations and organic/inorganic anions [10–12], which are diverse and changeable, making it feasible to precisely regulate the physico-chemical properties of ILs. In practice, ILs have been utilized for the preparation of CDs as carbon sources by microwave-assisted reaction, hydrothermal treatment as well as sulfuric acid carbonization [13–17]. By varying the cationic and anionic moieties, ILs mediated CDs (IL-CDs) show adjustable hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity [18,19], which provide much improved feature in the cell labelling and fluorescence imaging. In addition, IL-CDs exhibit favorable biocompatibility and better performance in drug delivery process [20,21]. In our previous studies, a series of imidazolium ionic liquids with various anionic moieties were used as carbon source for the preparation of carbon dots, where hydrophilic IL-HCDs and hydrophobic IL-OCDs were simultaneously obtained with hydrothermal treatment. The yields for IL-HCDs and IL-OCDs varied from 2.69% to 8.35% and 0–24.8% respectively [21]. In this respect, it is obviously limited for large scale production of the corresponding carbon dots and their practical applications.

Energetic ionic liquids (EILs) have appreciably developed as new energetic materials. The composition of EILs follows a distinct trend wherein nitrogen-rich (N-rich) moieties are involved, that is the cations of EILs generally include N-heteroaromatic rings or ammonium derivatives, while the anionic counterparts generally consist of azolates and dicyanamides [22–24]. In the present work, 1,3-dibutyl methylimidazolium dicyanamide is used as carbon source for the preparation of a novel ionic liquid mediated hydrophobic carbon dots (IL-OCDs), providing a surprisingly highyield of 58%. The IL-OCDs provide a high efficient medium for labelling yeast cell S. cerevisiae, which can be further concentrated with chitosan-modified Dy3+-doped Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles. Fluorescent detection of S. cerevisiae is thus facilitated with favorable sensitivity.

Ionic liquids 1,3-dibutyl methylimidazolium dicyanamide (BbimDCN) and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium dicyanamide (BmimDCN) are purchased from Shanghai Cheng Jie Chemical Co. LTD. Dysprosium oxide (Dy2O3), ferric chloride (FeCl3), ferrous chloride (FeCl2), trisodium citrate dihydrate (C6H3Na3O7·2H2O), chitosan, acetic acid (HAc), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and ethanol are obtained from Sino-pharm Chemical Reagent (Shenyang, China) and used as received. Deionized water of 18MΩcm−1 is used throughout the experiments. All the chemicals are at least of analytical reagent grade.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of the CDs are recorded on a JEM-2100 F microscope (JEOL, Japan) operating at 200kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra are obtained on a Nicolet-6700 spectrophotometer (Thermo Instruments Inc., USA) by using KBr pellets. UV–vis absorption spectra are recorded using a JASCO V-550 UV/visible spectrophotometer (JASCO International Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence spectra are performed on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) with 0.5cm quartz cuvette. The quantum yields of the CDs are recorded on a Quantaurus-QY absolute photoluminescence quantum yield measurement system (Hamamatsu Photonics, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra are conducted with an ESCALAB 250 surface analysis system (Thermo Instruments Inc, USA). As for the characterization of chitosan-modified dysprosium-doped magnetic iron nanocrystals, magnetic strength of the magnetic nanocrystals is measured with a 7400 vibrating sample magnetometer (Lakeshore). Yeast cell imaging studies are performed on a fluorescence micro-imaging system (UltraVIEW VoX, Perkin Elmer, America).

The IL-CDs are prepared by adding 1.0g of BbimDCN and 4.0mL of ethanol into a 25mL beaker, followed by reaction under microwave agitation at 1300W for 5min. The product is dissolved in ethanol, and then collected by centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 10min. The supernatant is dialyzed by molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 500 against ultrapure water for 24h. The hydrophilic IL-CDs (IL-HCDs) disperse in water and hydrophobic IL-CDs (IL-OCDs) gather in the inner surface of the dialysis bag. IL-OCDs, i.e., BbimDCN-OCDs, are used in the present study due to their higher imaging capacity. BbimDCN-OCDs are collected by decanting the aqueous solution and then further dialyzed against ethanol to remove the impurity and dried under vacuum. The obtained BbimDCN-OCDs are finally dissolved in ethanol for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging and growth curve of yeast cell incubated with BbimDCN-OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) cells are grown in yeast extract-peptone-dextrose (YPD) medium (10mg/mL yeast extract, 20mg/mL Bacto Peptone, 20mg/mL glucose, pH 6.5) at 28°C. The culturing medium is shaken at 150rpm for 13h. The S. cerevisiae cells in the middle of growth phase are collected by centrifugation at 7000rpm for 10min and used for the following experiments.

For cell imaging studies, the collected cells are re-suspended in 100mM of phosphate buffer containing 200μg/mL of BbimDCN-OCDs and 10% (v/v) of ethanol. After incubation at 28°C for 30min, the stained cells are washed for twice with PBS buffer and subject to confocal microscopic measurement.

The growth curve of S. cerevisiae is obtained by growing the cells in YPD medium containing various concentrations of BbimDCN-OCDs, e.g., 0.0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0mg/mL. During a 13-h incubation time, 2mL of the culture medium is collected in a 1-h interval and the optical density at 600nm (OD600) is monitored, followed by plotting the cell growth curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of chitosan-modified Dy3+-doped ferrite nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Dy3+-doped ferrite magnetic nanoparticles are prepared by adopting a chemical coprecipitation method with minor modifications [25]. Shortly, 42mL of Fe3+ solution (0.2mol/L), 8mL of Dy3+ solution (0.2mol/L), 50mL of trisodium citrate solution (6%, m/v) are added into a round bottom flask. The mixture is magnetically stirred at 90℃ for 20min, followed by addition of 50mL of Fe2+ solution (0.2mol/L). The reaction medium is adjusted to pH 11.0 with dilute NaOH solution (1.0mol/L) and continuously stirred for 1h. The medium is subsequently ripened for 20min and cooled down to room temperature. The nanoparticles are collected by applying an external magnet and thoroughly washed for five times with deionized water. Finally, the product is dried under vacuum at 70℃. Considering that the elemental molar ratio of Fe/Dy in the reaction mixture is set at 11/1, the Dy3+-doped ferrite magnetic nanoparticles are denoted as Fe2.75Dy0.25O4.

0.06g of the magnetic nanoparticles is added into 50mL of chitosan aqueous solution (0.8%, w/v). After stirring for 1h, the nanoparticles are magnetically collected and washed with deionized water for five times. The product chitosan-modified Dy3+-doped ferrite, denoted as Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4, is finally obtained.

2mL of S. cerevisiae yeast cell solution with various concentrations, e.g., 5.00×105, 1.00×105, 5.00 × 104, 1.00 × 104, 5.00 × 103, 1.00 × 103, 5.00 × 102, 1.00 × 102 CFU/mL, are added into centrifuge tubes. After centrifugation at 7000rpm for 10min, the supernatant is removed. Thereafter, PBS buffer, ethanol, BbimDCN-OCDs are sequentially added into each centrifuge tube with final concentrations of 0.01mol/L, 20% (v/v), 50μg/mL, respectively. Ethanol is used to improve the solubility of BbimDCN-OCDs, which facilitate labelling of the bacteria cells. After incubation at 28℃ for 30min, the cells in each centrifuge tube are washed and re-suspended into 0.01mol/L PBS. Finally, fluorescence emission intensity at λem = 440nm from the conjugated BbimDCN-OCDs in each cell suspension is recorded with excitation at 380nm. For the purpose of concentrating the yeast cells, they are first labelled with BbimDCN-OCDs, followed by adding 100μL of Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 solution (60mg/mL) to each centrifuge tube to perform magnetic recognition and separation with an external magnetic field.

To demonstrate the practical applications of the present method, it is further applied for real sample analysis, e.g., tap water and apple juice spiked with certain level of yeast S. cerevisiae. After labelling with BbimDCN-OCDs, Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 nanoparticles are added and incubated for 30min as described previously. The labelled yeast cells attached on the ferrite nanoparticles are then separated and collected by applying an external magnet for 10min and the remaining supernatant becomes transparent and clear. The collected bacteria-containing conjugate is thoroughly washed and afterwards subject to fluorescence detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of ionic liquid mediated BbimDCN-OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Our previous study indicated that the preparation of carbon dots with ionic liquids provides a number of advantages, while they generally give rise to an insufficient yield of production, i.e., 2.69–8.35% for IL-HCDs and 0–24.8% for IL-OCDs [21]. Considering the high-energy activity of energetic ionic liquids (EILs), the feasibility of ionic liquid 1,3-dibutyl methylimidazolium dicyanamide (BbimDCN), one of the EILs, as precusor for carbon dots preparation is investigated. The result demonstrates that BbimDCN offers a surprisingly high yield of 58%, which is far higher than those of the other IL-OCDs. To verify the superiority of DCN- anionic moiety in the preparation of IL-CDs, BmimDCN, with a same anion as BbimDCN while has a different cation, is utilized as precusor for IL-OCDs preparation with the same method. A favorable yield of 50% is achieved for BmimDCN-OCDs. These observations indicated that regardless of the cationic moiety, both BbimDCN-OCDs and BmimDCN-OCDs give rise to favorable high yield. This might be due to the fact that energetic DCN- moiety tend to decompose and thus facilitate the aggregation and carbonization of carbon source during the formation process of CDs.

It had been previously demonstrated that hydrophobic ionic liquid mediated carbon dots (IL-OCDs) exhibit better capacity for cell imaging with respect to their hydrophilic counterpart (IL-HCDs) owing to their faster penetration into HeLa cells [21]. Thus in the present study, the IL-OCDs, BbimDCN-OCDs, are collected and used for fluorescence labelling of S. cerevisiae in the ensuing studies.

HR-TEM image of the particle-size distribution and morphology of BbimDCN-OCDs is shown in Fig. 1A. The average particle size of BbimDCN-OCDs is derived to be 3.5 ± 0.4nm and it exhibits poor crystallinity which indicates their amorphous nature. UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 1B) illustrate that BbimDCN-OCDs exhibit a typical absorption at ca. 230nm, which is assigned to the π-π* transition of C˭C [26,27]. In addition, the excitation-wavelength dependent fluorescence behavior indicates an emission maximum at ca. 460nm with excitation at 380nm. Red-shift of the fluorescence maximum from 440 to 550nm is observed by varying the excitation wavelengths from 320nm to 460nm. The excitation-dependent red-shift emission is attributed to “defect states” with a broad energy distribution on the CDs [28]. A fluorescence quantum yield of 9.3% is achieved for the BbimDCN-OCDs carbon dots at excitation/emission wavelengths of λex/λem = 380nm/440nm.

In the FT-IR spectra of BbimDCN-OCDs ( Fig. 2A), the absorption band at 1164cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of -C˭O is clearly identified, confirming the presence of typical group of -C˭O on the surface of BbimDCN-OCDs [29]. Typical absorption of imidazole ring, i.e., vibration of the imidazole backbone at 1639cm−1, and characteristic absorption of cyanogroup, i.e., vibration of -C≡N at 2139cm−1, are all well identified in the spectra of both BmimBCN and BmimBCN-CDs [30]. This clearly demonstrated the presence of the precursor ionic liquid moiety, BbimDCN, on the surface of the obtained BbimDCN-OCDs.

The chemical composition and structure of BbimDCN-OCDs are further investigated by means of XPS. BbimDCN-OCDs show three strong peaks at around 285.0, 399.7 and 531.8eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s (as illustrated in Fig. 2B). The contents of C, N and O in BbimDCN-OCDs are derived to be 68.4%, 25.7% and 5.9%, respectively. The previous study indicated that N and O contents in the 1,3-dibutylimidazolium nitrate mediated hydrophobic CDs (BbimNO3-OCDs) are 5.16% and 10.48%, respectively [21]. The significantly higher content of nitrogen, along with a lower content of oxygen in the BbimDCN-OCDs demonstrates that more nitrogen dopes into BbimDCN-OCDs owing to the N-rich energetic precursor BbimDCN. High resolution XPS spectra of C1s for BbimDCN-OCDs exhibit three main peaks at 284.5, 285.5 and 286.5eV, which could be attributed to sp2 carbon (C-C, C˭C), sp3 carbon (C-N, C-O, C-S), C˭O carbon, respectively (Fig. 2C). N1s spectrum for BbimDCN-OCDs are deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.2eV, 400.5 and 401.2eV (Fig. 2D), which should be the contribution from pyridinic nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen and sp3 hybrid nitrogen (H-N). These results further demonstrate nitrogen doping into the BbimDCN-OCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Labelling of yeast cells with BbimDCN-OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent labelling of S. cerevisiae with BbimDCN-OCDs is firstly evaluated through confocal fluorescence microscopic imaging (as shown in Fig. 3). The yeast cells exhibit blue, green and red colour in dark field at excitation wavelengths of 405nm, 488nm and 561nm, respectively. It is obvious that each cell in the field of view is clearly labelled, which demonstrates the suitability of BbimDCN-OCDs serving as a potential alternative fluorescence probe for yeast imaging.

Considering the excellent labelling capacity of BbimDCN-OCDs, the content of S. cerevisiae in PBS buffer is detected with BbimDCN-OCDs as the probe. Excessive amount of BbimDCN-OCDs is mixed with various content of S. cerevisiae, which are afterwards collected by centrifugation and re-suspended for fluorescence detection. The fluorescence emission of BbimDCN-OCDs exhibits a maximum at 440nm with an excitation wavelength of 380nm. The control, i.e., yeast cells without labelling with BbimDCN-OCDs, shows no emission in the same experimental conditions, which clearly indicated that inherent fluorescence of S. cerevisiae causes no interference on the detection of yeast cells. The dependence of fluorescence intensity on the content of yeast cells in Fig. 4A shows a linear calibration graph of F = 3.37log[C]+5.42, within a range of 1 × 104−1 × 106 CFU/mL.

For the purpose of detecting microorganisms, favorable biocompatibility for the BbimDCN-OCDs is desirable. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of BbimDCN-OCDs is investigated by measuring the yeast growth curves (as shown in Fig. 5). After incubating S. cerevisiae with the carbon dots BbimDCN-OCDs within the range of 0–1.0mg/mL, the growth curves of the yeast cells remain virtually the same as that of the control. This observation indicated that the presence of 1.0mg/mL BbimDCN-OCDs has no obvious effect on the growth of S. cerevisiae. In practical applications, 50μg/mL of BbimDCN-OCDs suffices the fluorescence detection.

Considering that collection of S. cerevisiae by centrifugation is generally quite time-consuming, while Dy3+-doped magnetic nanoparticles, Fe2.75Dy0.25O4, could help to isolate/collect the yeast cells after labelling with the carbon dots. The saturation magnetization and magnetic induction can be improved by doping Dy3+. Chitosan is a cationic heteropolysaccharide composed mainly of β-(1,4)−2-deoxy-2-amino-glucopyranoseunits, and partially of β-(1,4)−2-deoxy-2-acetamido-glucopyranose. The modification/encapsulation of chitosan on Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 nanoparticles could improve the selectivity of S. cerevisiae due to the electrostatic attraction between the positively charged chitosan and negatively charged S. cerevisiae.

Fig. 6A shows TEM image of the magnetic nanoparticles Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4. It is clearly seen that the nanoparticles appear to be spherical with a size of 7.2 ± 0.7nm. The substitution of dysprosium cation has no obvious influence on the morphology of the ferrite nanoparticles. FT-IR spectra of chitosan, Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 and Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 are illustrated in Fig. 6B. The absorption bands at 2923cm−1 and 775cm−1 are identified in the spectrum of chitosan while not found in that of Fe2.75Dy0.25O4. These absorptions bands are assigned to the stretching and bending vibrations of the CH groups, respectively. After encapsulation of chitosan, they appear in the spectrum of Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4. These results well indicate the encapsulation of chitosan on the surface of the Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 nanoparticles.

In order to understand the effect of Dy3+ and chitosan on the magnetic properties of the Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 composite, magnetization measurements are carried out on a vibrating sample magnetometer at room temperature with a maximum applied field of 10kOe. The magnetization curve of Fe3O4, Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 and Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 are shown in Fig. 7A. All of these magnetic iron nanocrystals exhibit typical S-shape curve, illustrating the characteristic of superparamagnetism. In addition, the saturation magnetization (Ms) value of Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 increases from 45.39 to 50.12emu/g after the doping of Dy3+. The increment of magnetism would accelerate the aggregation of Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 nanoparticles and thus minimize the separation time. On the other hand, the Ms value for Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 decreases to 35.04emu/g after the encapsulation of chitosan.

The surface charge variation for chitosan, Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 and Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 are shown in Fig. 7B. The zeta potential for chitosan is 27.8mV due to the existence of abundant –NH2 groups. That of Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 is 2.3mV, and the encapsulation/modification of positively charged chitosan results in a dramatic increase of the zeta potential to 43.5mV for Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4. This result further proves that the encapsulation of chitosan should facilitate the selective absorption of negatively charged microorganisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Labelling and detection of S. cerevisiae
ce:section.ce:para: Given the strong fluorescence of BbimDCN-OCDs and high capture efficiency of the Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4 nanoparticles, a novel scheme for the quantification of S. cerevisiae is developed as illustrated in Scheme 1. BbimDCN-OCDs are first mixed with the sample solution for the labelling of S. cerevisiae. Therein the bacteria cells are selectively adhered onto the magnetic Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4. Thereafter the S. cerevisiae cells are separated and collected with an external magnetic and finally subject to fluorescence measurements.

In practice, various contents of S. cerevisiae (5 × 102−1 × 106 CFU/mL) are mixed with 50μg/mL of BbimDCN-OCDs and 3mg/mL of Chitosan@Fe2.75Dy0.25O4. After appropriate incubation as described in the Experimental section, the carbon dots BbimDCN-OCDs labelled yeast cells are isolated by using an external magnet. Thereafter, the fluorescence intensity at λex/λem = 380nm/440nm of carbon dots labelled in S. cerevisiae is measured. The calibration curve is shown in Fig. 4B, providing a linear regression equation of F = 3.01log[C]+11.7, within a linear range of 5 × 102−1 × 106 CFU/mL along with a detection limit of 5 × 102 CFU/mL. The experimental results have indicated that after magnetic adhesion and concentration, the sensitivity for S. cerevisiae detection is at least of two orders of magnitude more sensitive than that using BbimDCN-OCDs alone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacteria quantification in real sample matrixes
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the applicability of the proposed method, it is employed for the detection of S. cerevisiae content in different sample matrixes, including tap water, apple juice. Considering that S. cerevisiae content is generally less than 100 CFU/mL or below the detection limit in these samples, S. cerevisiae at concentration levels of 1 × 103 and 1 × 105 CFU/mL is spiked into the samples and then recoveries are derived. The analytical results are listed in Table 1. These results indicated that the present method is beneficial to the enrichment of few microorganisms and improvement of the detection sensitivity.

In this work, a high yield of hydrophobic carbon dots is achieved with energetic ionic liquid as carbon source. Chitosan-modified Dy3+-doped magnetite with superparamagnetism is prepared for exclusive recognition and concentration of S. cerevisiae. The doping of Dy3+ increases the magnetism and the encapsulation of chitosan improves the selectivity to S. cerevisiae. An efficient detection method for S. cerevisiae is thus developed, wherein the bacteria cells are labelled with carbon dots for facilitating their ensuing fluorescence detection, while the magnetic enrichment improves the detection sensitivity. The present work opens a new avenue for the development of analytical protocols for specific analytical and bioimaging purposes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21575020, 21727811), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N152004005), National Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates (201710145109).
